On disclosures and notifications, the EDPS finds that additional contractual provisions now ensure that only EU or Member State law, or third-country law providing essentially equivalent protection, may require Microsoft or sub-processors to omit notification of disclosure requests or to disclose personal data processed within and outside the EEA.

The EDPS received a compliance report from the Commission in December 2024 and held subsequent discussions with Commission services before receiving the Commission's letter of 3 July 2025 detailing additional measures implemented and scheduled by both the Commission and Microsoft. The EDPS notes that the closure of these proceedings relates to specific provisions of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 examined in the 2024 Decision and does not constitute an assessment of the Commission's overall compliance with other provisions of the Regulation. The Commission has made the compliance improvements to the Inter-Institutional Licensing Agreement with Microsoft available to other EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies contracting Microsoft 365 under that agreement.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 Proposal and NIS 2 Directive Amendments, Citing Data Protection Concerns
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor, acting jointly under Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, have adopted Joint Opinion 4/2026 on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and amendments to the NIS 2 Directive, raising recommendations on personal data processing safeguards, ENISA's expanded role, and incident reporting mechanisms.

On 18 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted their joint opinion following the Commission's formal consultation of 21 January 2026. The Commission had on 20 January 2026 issued a proposal for a Regulation on the EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), covering the European cybersecurity certification framework and ICT supply chain security, alongside a proposal to amend Directive (EU) 2022/2555 through simplification measures aligned with the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the relationship between data protection and cybersecurity is double-sided: cybersecurity measures serve to protect personal data by limiting risks of unwanted access, modification or unavailability, yet certain cybersecurity measures can interfere with individuals' rights to privacy and data protection. The joint opinion notes that necessity and proportionality are required considerations alongside effectiveness. The EDPB and EDPS support the proposals' objective to strengthen ENISA's role and facilitate cybersecurity certification uptake, subject to the specific recommendations set out in the opinion, and welcome the objective of establishing mechanisms to make compliance with cybersecurity requirements more coherent.

The EDPB and EDPS strongly support the proposed single-entry point for personal data breach notifications (Art. 15 CSA2), finding that this would reduce administrative burden for organisations without diminishing protection for data subjects. The joint opinion recommends that the EDPS be added explicitly as a Union body with which ENISA would cooperate, and as a possible requestor of advice from ENISA, ensuring clear coordination and division of responsibilities. The EDPB and EDPS further find that where ENISA's future tasks as an information hub would require substantial processing of personal data, the essential elements of any large-scale processing and appropriate safeguards are required to be set out explicitly in the basic act governing those tasks. The joint opinion also recommends that Art. 66 of the CSA2 proposal be amended to provide for prior consultation with the EDPS before adoption of internal rules on personal data processing by ENISA's Management Board.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Orientations on Generative AI and Personal Data Protection for EU Institutions
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its first Orientations on generative artificial intelligence and personal data protection, providing EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies with practical guidance on processing personal data when using generative AI systems under the applicable EU data protection framework.

On 3 June 2024, the European Data Protection Supervisor published the document titled "First EDPS Orientations for EUIs using Generative AI", addressed to EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, referred to collectively as EUIs, with the stated purpose of facilitating compliance with data protection requirements when those entities deploy or interact with generative AI systems.

The EDPS states that the Orientations are intended to provide practical advice and instructions on the processing of personal data in the context of generative AI use. The document, published in English and running to a file size of 5.17 MB, covers the intersection of generative AI capabilities and the obligations EUIs hold under the EU data protection legal framework, which for EUIs is principally Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 rather than the GDPR. The EDPS finds that EUIs require dedicated operational guidance given the particular legal framework governing their data processing activities, distinct from that applicable to private sector entities or EU member state public bodies.

The EDPS states that the Orientations address how personal data may be affected at various stages of generative AI system use, including during input, processing and output phases. The EDPS frames the document as a compliance facilitation tool, recognising that generative AI systems present novel questions about data minimisation, purpose limitation and accountability obligations applicable to EUIs (Arts. 4, 5 and 26 Regulation (EU) 2018/1725). The EDPS notes that the publication follows a period of increased institutional deployment of generative AI tools across the EU administration and reflects the EDPS's supervisory interest in ensuring that such deployments are assessed against existing data protection requirements before and during use.

rows.title: EDPS adopts decision requiring interest representatives to be registered in EU Transparency Register before meetings with senior staff
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has adopted a formal decision requiring that the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and all staff holding managerial responsibilities engage, in their official capacities, exclusively with interest representatives registered in the EU Transparency Register, with meeting information to be published publicly.

On 24 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted its Decision on Transparency Measures in accordance with the Interinstitutional Agreement of 20 May 2021 between the European Parliament, the Council of the European Union and the European Commission on a mandatory transparency register, with the decision published on the EDPS website on 20 April 2026. The EDPS notes that no financial penalty is associated with this decision.

The decision, adopted with reference to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 on the processing of personal data by Union institutions and bodies, and to the Interinstitutional Agreement at Article 11 thereof, establishes that scheduled bilateral encounters organised to discuss policy or legislation falling within EDPS competencies constitute meetings for the purposes of the new framework. The EDPS finds it appropriate that only those interest representatives registered in the Transparency Register, established under the 2021 Interinstitutional Agreement, qualify as eligible counterparts for official engagement by the Supervisor and senior staff.

The decision further requires that information on meetings held between the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and staff holding management functions with interest representatives, including participants and subjects addressed, be published in the EDPS Agenda. The EDPS states that this publication obligation is intended to enhance institutional accountability and foster public understanding of the authority's operations. The EDPS also notes that the decision is intended to lay down the foundations for an improved transparency policy for the EDPS as a Union entity.

To complement the decision, the EDPS has made available a Public Access to Documents Register, allowing the public to consult documents for which access has been granted, alongside resources explaining the process for exercising public access rights under the applicable framework.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed EPPO and OLAF Access to VAT Information Under Amended Regulation (EU) No 904/2010
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued Opinion 1/2026, finding that proposed direct access by the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office to VAT information is necessary but requires additional data protection safeguards, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 (Art. 42(1) EUDPR).

On 7 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 1/2026 on the European Commission's Proposal for a Council Regulation amending Regulation (EU) No 904/2010 (COM(2025) 685 final), which was itself issued on 14 November 2025. The Proposal seeks to grant the European Public Prosecutor's Office (EPPO) and the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF) direct and centralised access to VAT information at Union level, with the stated aim of combating Missing Trader Intra-Community (MTIC) fraud.

The EDPS finds that the proposed access is necessary to achieve the objective of effectively combating cross-border VAT fraud, noting the targeted nature of the access and the specific categories of information concerned. The EDPS states that these categories relate primarily to VAT numbers and business-to-business transactions. However, the EDPS stresses that personal data processing for administrative purposes and for criminal law enforcement purposes are legally distinct, and the EDPS finds that this separation is required to be carefully reflected in the Proposal. The EDPS further finds that the Proposal is required to clearly explain the exceptional nature of granting law enforcement bodies direct access to administrative databases, and the EDPS considers that further clarification is warranted to prevent the measure being misconstrued as a precedent for broader application.

On technical and organisational safeguards, the EDPS determines that certain parameters governing data access are required to be defined at the level of the basic act itself, rather than left entirely to implementing acts as currently envisaged. The EDPS recommends that the Proposal incorporate additional safeguards to ensure compliance with the principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accountability under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS also calls for ex-post evaluation and review mechanisms to be included in the legislative text to enable ongoing oversight of the measure's data protection implications.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act Proposal, Raising Data Protection Concerns
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor, in Joint Opinion 3/2026 adopted on 10 March 2026, have identified areas where the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act requires amendment to ensure compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 in response to the European Commission's Proposal of 16 December 2025 for a Regulation establishing a framework for strengthening the Union's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors. The opinion addresses proposed amendments to the Clinical Trials Regulation, the Food Law Regulation, and the SoHO Regulation.

The EDPB and EDPS find that proposed amendments to Article 93 of the Clinical Trials Regulation require clarification on legal bases under Article 6 GDPR and the application of Article 9(2)(i) and (j) GDPR to special categories of data. The authorities find that the qualification of sponsors and investigators as controllers requires further precision to ensure accountability obligations are properly allocated. The EDPB and EDPS determine that proposed retention periods for personal data, including genetic data, and conditions for further processing for scientific research necessitate additional safeguards. The authorities find that the current drafting on technical and organisational measures under Article 32 GDPR is insufficient.

On provisions addressing artificial intelligence as a biotechnology enabler, the EDPB and EDPS find that verification of legitimate need requires alignment with existing GDPR principles, and that provisions governing AI use in clinical trials must be brought into conformity with data protection requirements. The authorities state that the identified provisions require amendment before adoption.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Commission Proposal to Amend GDPR Record-Keeping Obligations for SMEs and SMCs
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have issued a Joint Opinion welcoming, with qualifications, the European Commission's proposed amendment to the record-keeping derogation under Art. 30(5) GDPR, whilst requesting clarifications on the employee threshold and the scope of the term 'organisation'.

On 9 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Joint Opinion 01/2025 on the Commission's Proposal for a Regulation amending certain regulations, including the GDPR, as part of the fourth simplification Omnibus package. No fines are at issue in this opinion.

The Proposal seeks to modify Art. 30(5) GDPR, which provides a derogation from the obligation to maintain records of processing activities. The EDPB and EDPS note that the current derogation applies only to enterprises and organisations with fewer than 250 employees, subject to exceptions. Under the Commission's Proposal, the threshold would be extended to enterprises or organisations employing fewer than 750 persons, unless the processing is likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms within the meaning of Art. 35 GDPR. The Proposal also introduces definitions of SME and small mid-cap enterprise (SMC) into Art. 4 GDPR, and extends the scope of Art. 40(1) and Art. 42(1) GDPR, covering codes of conduct and certification, to SMCs.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposed modifications to Art. 30(5) are targeted and limited in nature and do not affect the core principles and obligations under the GDPR. Both bodies support the general objective of reducing administrative burden for SMEs and SMCs, provided that the protection of individuals' fundamental rights, including the rights to privacy and to the protection of personal data, is not diminished. The Joint Opinion notes, however, that the exemption in Art. 30(5) as drafted refers to 'enterprises employing fewer than 750 employees' without cross-referencing the newly introduced definitions of SME and SMC in Art. 4 GDPR, which also incorporate financial criteria. The EDPB and EDPS recommend that the provision refer to those definitions to ensure the exemption benefits only the intended categories of entity.

The Joint Opinion further requests that co-legislators clarify why a threshold of 750 employees is more appropriate under the GDPR than the 500-employee threshold initially considered, given that the Proposal affects legislation across other policy areas. The EDPB and EDPS also request that co-legislators clarify that the term 'organisation' within the scope of the proposed derogation under Art. 30(5) GDPR does not include public authorities and bodies.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Directive Amendments
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted amendments to the NIS2 Directive, broadly supporting the cybersecurity package while recommending stronger coordination with data protection authorities under Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 20 January 2026, the European Commission published its cybersecurity package proposal, and on 21 January 2026 formally consulted the EDPB and the EDPS, requesting a joint opinion in accordance with Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The joint opinion was adopted on 19 March 2026.

Regarding the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal, the EDPB and EDPS support the general objective of strengthening the role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and facilitating uptake of cybersecurity certification. The joint opinion welcomes the clarification that ENISA's advice would be issued upon a prior request from the EDPB, establishing a clear division of responsibilities, and recommends that the EDPS be added as a possible requestor of ENISA advice. The joint opinion also recommends that where ENISA's Management Board adopts additional measures relating to the processing of personal data, such decisions be limited to technical details, and that the EDPS be consulted prior to adoption of such rules. The EDPB and EDPS further recommend that an explicit reference to the EDPS be added as an EU body with which ENISA cooperates.

On certification, the joint opinion finds that the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification. The EDPB and EDPS determine that ENISA is required to consult the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to the security of personal data processing, and that such schemes for products, services and processes likely used in data processing operations take into account security controls capable of demonstrating compliance with GDPR requirements. The joint opinion also supports establishing a single-entry point for notification of personal data breaches, consistent with the earlier EDPB-EDPS joint opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation Proposal, on the basis that this would reduce administrative burden without diminishing protection for individuals. Regarding the NIS2 amendments, the EDPB and EDPS welcome the designation of European Digital Identity Wallet and European Business Wallet providers as essential entities.

rows.title: EDPS Completes Ten Prior Consultations for Europol and Eurojust Within Legal Deadlines in 2025
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor concluded ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust within applicable legal deadlines, confirming the mechanism as a binding oversight tool under EU data protection law for high-risk processing in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published details of its prior consultation practice in 2025, covering processing operations by Europol and Eurojust. The EDPS assessed and concluded all ten consultations within prescribed timeframes: six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation.

The EDPS finds that prior consultation obligations arise where planned processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, even after mitigation measures are identified. The EDPS notes that the legal basis derives from Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, Directive (EU) 2016/680, and sector-specific regulations governing Europol and Eurojust. The EDPS determines that consultation is required where a Data Protection Impact Assessment indicates high risk or where processing involves high risk through new technologies, mechanisms or procedures.

The EDPS finds that prior consultations provide legal certainty to controllers by delivering guidance before high-risk processing commences, enable proactive risk identification, and facilitate harmonised interpretation of data protection principles. The authority notes that, following examination of a proposed operation, it issues a written opinion recommending modifications or safeguards, giving controllers actionable guidance before processing begins.

The EDPS sets out the urgency procedure under Europol's regulation. Where processing is particularly urgent to prevent crime or protect vital interests, the EDPS notes that Europol is permitted to initiate processing after launching prior consultation but before the standard deadline expires. Europol is required to notify the EDPS before starting processing, and the EDPS's advice is required to be considered, with processing adjusted where necessary. The EDPS finds this procedure preserves independent supervision without compromising operational capacity.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus AI Proposal Calls for Retained Safeguards on High-Risk Systems and Data Processing
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal, supporting administrative simplification in principle whilst finding that several proposed changes risk undermining fundamental rights protections under the AI Act.

On 21 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published their Joint Opinion on the Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal, which seeks to streamline implementation of certain harmonised rules under the AI Act. No financial penalties are at issue in this opinion.

The Joint Opinion finds that the proposal's extension of permissions to process special categories of personal data, including ethnicity and health data, for bias detection and correction to providers and deployers of any AI systems and models requires further restriction. The EDPB and EDPS recommend specifying that such data may be used for bias detection and correction only in circumscribed situations where the risk of adverse effects from bias is considered sufficiently serious. On registration obligations, the Joint Opinion advises against the proposed deletion of the requirement to register AI systems classified as high-risk even where providers self-assess their systems as non-high-risk, finding that this change would undermine accountability and create an incentive for providers to claim exemptions to avoid public scrutiny.

Regarding EU-level AI regulatory sandboxes, the EDPB and EDPS welcome their creation but recommend the direct involvement of competent data protection authorities in supervising data processing within those sandboxes. The Joint Opinion further recommends that the EDPB be afforded an advisory role and observer status at the European Artificial Intelligence Board to ensure consistency at EU level. The EDPB and EDPS state that the supervisory role of the AI Office with respect to AI systems based on general-purpose AI models must be clearly delineated and must not overlap with the independent supervisory function of the EDPS over AI systems developed or used by Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies. The EDPB and EDPS also recommend maintaining a duty on AI providers and deployers to ensure AI literacy among staff, and express concern over the proposed postponement of core provisions applicable to high-risk AI systems, inviting co-legislators to consider whether the original timeline can be maintained for obligations including transparency requirements.

rows.title: EDPS Opinion on UN Convention against Cybercrime: EU Fundamental Rights Protections Must Be Preserved in International Data Transfers
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor issued an Opinion on 4 September 2025 on two Council proposals concerning the signing and conclusion, on behalf of the European Union, of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime, finding that international law enforcement cooperation under the Convention must remain consistent with EU fundamental rights guarantees on data protection and privacy, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 4 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 23/2025, addressing Commission proposals to authorise both the signing and the conclusion of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime on the EU's behalf. The EDPS notes that the Convention is designed to establish common global rules for preventing and combating cybercrime and for collecting electronic evidence in criminal investigations and proceedings.

The EDPS welcomes the Convention's provision that States Parties are not obliged to transfer personal data where such transfers would breach their applicable data protection laws. The EDPS underlines, however, that EU Member States, when implementing and applying the Convention, are required to verify in each individual case whether the conditions set out in Chapter V of the Law Enforcement Directive are satisfied before transferring personal data to a third country. The EDPS notes the heterogeneous nature of the legal systems of United Nations member states with respect to fundamental rights protections, and the EDPS finds that this heterogeneity makes case-by-case verification particularly critical.

The EDPS further finds that competent authorities of Member States are required to ensure that personal data transfers to third-country parties to the Convention remain fully consistent with international human rights obligations and the fundamental rights of the individuals concerned. The EDPS notes that where those conditions are not met, Member States have grounds available to refuse cooperation and are expected to exercise them. The EDPS also recommends that the practical effects of the Convention be carefully assessed once in operation, and that data protection experts be involved in any future reviews of the Convention's text. The EDPS additionally finds that any future proposals to introduce offences incompatible with EU law or values within the Convention framework are to be firmly opposed.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed EU Return Regulation, Citing Data Protection Risks for Migrants and Children
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published Opinion 9/2025 on the European Commission's proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU, identifying a series of data protection concerns and recommending amendments to the text.

On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor issued its opinion following the Commission's publication on 11 March 2025 of the proposal (COM(2025) 101 final), which aims to complement the legislative framework of the Pact on Migration and Asylum adopted in May 2024 and to repeal Directive 2008/115/EC, Council Directive 2001/40/EC and Council Decision 2004/191/EC. No financial penalty is associated with this opinion.

The EDPS acknowledges the need for more effective enforcement of EU and national laws in migration and asylum but finds that data protection represents one of the last lines of defence for vulnerable individuals, including migrants and asylum seekers approaching EU external borders. The EDPS considers that the proposed comprehensive approach is required to be based on full respect for the fundamental rights of persons seeking international protection and other migrants, including their rights to data protection and privacy under the applicable Union framework. In view of the proposal's impact on those fundamental rights, the EDPS finds that an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment is warranted.

The EDPS recommends aligning the information about data subjects' rights to be provided under Article 24 of the proposal with the relevant provisions of already adopted legal acts in the area of migration and asylum. The EDPS further finds that the limitation of information about the factual reasons for a return decision to the essence of the grounds on which it is taken must not be applied as a rule but only in exceptional cases where strictly necessary, such as where disclosure would be contrary to State security interests, in order to preserve access to an effective remedy. The EDPS also highlights the need for additional safeguards where data relating to criminal convictions of third-country nationals are transferred to third countries, and recommends that the conditions under which personal data of children may be transferred to the third country of return be further specified in the text.

rows.title: European Commission Proposes DMA Compliance Measures Requiring Google to Share Search Data with Third Parties
rows.summary:
The European Commission has sent preliminary findings to Google proposing measures under the Digital Markets Act (DMA) requiring the company to share search engine data with third-party search engines on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.

On 16 April 2026, the European Commission issued proposed measures to Google addressing its obligations as a gatekeeper under the DMA. The proposals set out how Google is required to provide third-party search engines with access to search data, including ranking, query, click and view data, on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.

The Commission determines that the proposed measures cover six areas: the eligibility of data beneficiaries to receive search data, including artificial intelligence chatbots with search functionalities; the scope of search data Google is required to share; the means and frequency of data sharing; measures to ensure the anonymisation of personal data; parameters for setting fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory prices for search data; and processes governing beneficiaries' access to search data. The Commission finds that the aim of these measures is to enable third-party online search engines to optimise their search services and contest Google Search's position in the market.

To assess the effectiveness of the proposed measures, the Commission has opened a public consultation inviting interested parties to submit comments, with the consultation opening on 17 April 2026. The Commission's preliminary findings do not constitute a final determination of non-compliance, and the proposed measures remain subject to revision following the consultation process. The Commission notes that the eligibility criteria for data beneficiaries extend to AI-enabled services with search functionalities, reflecting the Commission's assessment of the evolving competitive landscape in online search.

rows.title: EU Special Panel on Child Online Safety Convenes Second Meeting, Reviewing Age Verification App and International Approaches
rows.summary:
The Special Panel on child online safety, advising European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, convened its second meeting on 16 April 2026 to examine existing EU regulatory frameworks and international measures aimed at protecting minors online.

On 16 April 2026, the Special Panel met to review current EU rules and initiatives relevant to child online safety, drawing on expertise from advisers to Member States. The meeting also examined international approaches, including Australia's legislated minimum age requirement for social media access. The session followed the Commission's presentation, on 15 April 2026, of a finalised EU age verification application, which the Commission describes as the most privacy-preserving and user-friendly tool of its kind for protecting children online.

The panel's discussion encompassed a range of existing EU instruments and the degree to which they address the protection of minors across online platforms. Experts advising Member States contributed analysis of national and cross-border measures, enabling the panel to assess gaps and areas of alignment. The Commission's age verification application was presented as a harmonised European solution, with President von der Leyen stating that Member States are encouraged to begin customising the application so that it can be made available to citizens. The Commission describes the application as meeting what it characterises as the highest privacy standards globally.

President von der Leyen stated that protecting children online requires a harmonised European approach, and that the age verification tool is intended to assist parents in protecting children across platforms. The Commission's position, as reflected in the panel's proceedings, is that coordinated EU-level action is necessary to achieve consistent protection for minors in the digital environment. The Special Panel continues to advise the President in its capacity as an expert body, and its work feeds into the Commission's broader policy development on child online safety under applicable EU frameworks, including the Digital Services Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065).

rows.title: EDPB adopts Opinion 11/2026 on Belgian SA draft decision approving Kuwait Petroleum Group Controller BCRs under Art. 47 GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted Opinion 11/2026 under Article 64(1)(f) GDPR, assessing the Belgian supervisory authority's draft decision to approve the Controller Binding Corporate Rules of the Kuwait Petroleum Group as a transfer mechanism for international personal data flows.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 11/2026, exercising its function under Article 63 and Article 64(1)(f) GDPR to ensure consistent application of the regulation across the European Economic Area where a supervisory authority proposes to approve Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) within the meaning of Article 47 GDPR. No financial penalty is associated with this opinion.

The EDPB notes that, pursuant to Article 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Article 45(3) GDPR, a controller may transfer personal data to a third country only where appropriate safeguards are in place and where enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The EDPB finds that a group of undertakings engaged in a joint economic activity may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and satisfy the requirements set out in Article 47(2) GDPR. The EDPB states that the opinion evaluates whether the Kuwait Petroleum Group's submitted BCRs meet those requirements and align with EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the application for approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller BCRs, adopted on 20 June 2023, which the EDPB notes supersede Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264.

The EDPB states that the scope of the opinion is confined to consideration of whether the BCRs afford appropriate safeguards as required by Article 47 GDPR and the Recommendations. The EDPB notes that the opinion does not address other GDPR obligations referenced within the BCRs, nor does it address any supplementary measures that an exporter subject to the GDPR may be required to adopt depending on the circumstances of a transfer, in accordance with the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18. The EDPB further recalls that responsibility for assessing whether supplementary measures are necessary in any given transfer context rests with the exporter.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Opinion 9/2026 on Dutch SA Draft Decision Approving Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group Controller BCRs
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted Opinion 9/2026 under Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, reviewing the Dutch Supervisory Authority's draft decision on the controller Binding Corporate Rules submitted by the Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group for approval as an international data transfer mechanism.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 9/2026 pursuant to Art. 63 and Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, which requires the EDPB to issue an opinion where a supervisory authority aims to approve Binding Corporate Rules within the meaning of Art. 47 GDPR. The opinion concerns the draft decision of the Dutch Supervisory Authority, acting as BCR Lead, regarding the controller BCRs of the Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group. The EDPB notes that no financial penalties are associated with this opinion.

The EDPB notes that, pursuant to Art. 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Art. 45(3) GDPR, a controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country only where appropriate safeguards are provided and enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The EDPB finds that a group of undertakings may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and fulfil the requirements set out in Art. 47(2) GDPR. The EDPB further notes that BCRs are intended to provide guarantees applying uniformly across all third countries, independently of the level of protection guaranteed in each jurisdiction.

The EDPB states that the scope of Opinion 9/2026 is limited to consideration of whether the BCRs submitted afford appropriate safeguards by meeting all requirements of Art. 47 GDPR and the EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the Application for Approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller Binding Corporate Rules, adopted on 20 June 2023, which the EDPB confirms supersede Article 29 Working Party working documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The EDPB states that the opinion does not address other GDPR obligations referenced in the BCRs beyond those relating to Art. 47 GDPR, nor does it address any supplementary measures an exporter subject to the GDPR may be required to adopt depending on the circumstances of specific transfers. The EDPB also recalls, by reference to the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18, that responsibility for compliance with transfer obligations rests with the exporting entity.

rows.title: Court of Justice of the European Union Hears Challenge to Belgian Law on Blanket Retention of Communications Data
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union heard oral argument on 14 April 2026 in Case C-661/24, Académie Fiscale and Others, a preliminary reference concerning whether Belgian legislation requiring blanket retention of traffic and location data from all electronic communications users is compatible with EU law on privacy and personal data protection.

On 14 April 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union convened a hearing in Case C-661/24, referred by the Belgian Constitutional Court. The case concerns a Belgian law obliging operators of electronic communications services to retain traffic and location data from all users for a limited period, with the stated aims of preventing fraud and malicious use of networks and ensuring network security. No fine is at issue in this reference proceeding.

The Belgian Constitutional Court has asked the Court of Justice to determine whether national legislation of this character, requiring operators to retain certain categories of communications data across the entirety of a user base rather than in relation to targeted individuals or groups, is permissible under EU law. The referring court's question centres on the tension between the operational objectives cited by the Belgian legislature and the rights to privacy and the protection of personal data recognised under EU law, including under the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

Several professional organisations, fundamental rights associations and individuals brought proceedings before the Belgian Constitutional Court, arguing that the law results in overly broad data retention affecting all users of electronic communications services. The claimants contend that such a generalised retention regime undermines the right to private life and the right to the protection of personal data. The Court of Justice has previously addressed the compatibility of general and indiscriminate data retention obligations with EU law in a line of cases including La Quadrature du Net and Others, and the present reference invites the Court to assess how those principles apply to a retention scheme justified specifically by network security and anti-fraud objectives rather than national security grounds.
rows.body_name: General Court of the European Union

rows.title: Europol Coordinates Global Operation PowerOFF Action Week Targeting 75,000 DDoS-for-Hire Users Across 21 Countries
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation coordinated a global enforcement action week under Operation PowerOFF, resulting in 4 arrests, the takedown of 53 domains, and the issuing of 25 search warrants targeting distributed denial-of-service-for-hire infrastructure across 21 participating countries.

On 13 April 2026, the European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation announced the conclusion of the coordinated action week, which directed enforcement and prevention measures against over 75,000 identified criminal users of DDoS-for-hire services. No financial penalties are reported in connection with this action. Participating countries included Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Bulgaria, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Japan, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States.

Europol finds that leading up to the action week, a series of operational sprints gathered experts from national authorities to act against high-value target users of DDoS-for-hire platforms, referred to as booter services. Europol determined that seized databases enabled Europol experts to provide national counterparts with data on over 3 million criminal user accounts, which in turn supported the coordinated actions carried out during the action week. Europol's operational support included analysis of seized datasets, identification and geolocation of users, dissemination of intelligence packages to all EU Member States and countries with operational agreements, crypto-tracing expertise, forensic assistance, and the establishment of a command post during the action days.

Europol further finds that DDoS-for-hire is among the most accessible forms of cybercrime, enabling individuals with limited technical knowledge to execute attacks using step-by-step tutorials. Europol notes that motivations among users range from curiosity and hacktivism to financial gain through extortion or disruption of competitors. As Operation PowerOFF entered its prevention phase, Europol coordinated campaigns including the creation of targeted search engine advertisements directed at young people seeking DDoS-for-hire tools, the removal of over 100 URLs advertising such services from search engine results, and the transmission of warning messages via blockchains used by criminals to process payments related to their illegal activities.
rows.body_name: European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation

rows.title: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights publishes Article 52 Charter page on scope and interpretation of rights and principles
rows.summary:
The EU Agency for Fundamental Rights has published a dedicated reference page for Article 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, which sets out the scope and interpretation of the Charter's rights and principles, including the conditions under which limitations on those rights are permissible under EU law.

On 10 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights made available its page for Article 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, which governs the scope and interpretation of the Charter's rights and principles.

The Charter provides that any limitation on the exercise of rights and freedoms recognised by the Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essential content of those rights and freedoms. The Charter states that, subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations are permissible only where they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or protect the rights and freedoms of others. The Charter determines that where Charter rights correspond to rights guaranteed by the European Convention on Human Rights, their meaning and scope are the same as those laid down by that Convention, whilst Union law may provide more extensive protection.

The FRA page also addresses the distinction between rights and principles within the Charter. The Charter provides that provisions containing principles may be implemented by legislative and executive acts of Union institutions and member states when implementing Union law, and their invocation before a court is admissible only for interpreting and reviewing the legality of such acts. The FRA notes that determining whether a given matter falls within the scope of Union law, as required by the Charter, is not straightforward in practice.

rows.title: EU Commission publishes first monitoring results under revised Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online+
rows.summary:
The European Commission has published the results of the first monitoring exercise conducted under the revised Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online+, covering five signatory platforms and incorporating both independent reporter data and platform self-assessments, within the co-regulatory framework of the Digital Services Act (Art. 45 DSA).

On 10 April 2026, the European Commission published the findings from the inaugural monitoring cycle under the revised Code+, which ran from early November to mid-December 2025. The Commission formally endorsed the Code as a code of conduct within the meaning of Article 45 DSA on 20 January 2025, at the request of signatories, integrating it into the DSA co-regulatory framework along with the European Board for Digital Services.

The monitoring exercise, structured as a mystery-shopping stress-test of up to six weeks, assessed whether signatory platforms reviewed the majority, defined as at least 50 per cent, of notifications from monitoring reporters within 24 hours. Of the 12 signatories to the Code+, only five, namely Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, X and YouTube, received relevant notifications from monitoring reporters during the reporting period. The Commission finds that these platforms maintained their commitment to reviewing the majority of notifications within 24 hours and provided systematic feedback to reporters. However, the Commission also finds that all platforms covered by the exercise, with the exception of X, classified a considerable share of cases as either disputed or errors. The Commission notes that a significant proportion of error cases appears attributable to incorrect reporting channels used by monitoring reporters.

Seven of the 12 signatories, Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, X and Snapchat, have been designated as Very Large Online Platforms or Very Large Online Search Engines under the DSA on the basis of having 45 million or more monthly users in the EU. The Commission states that for these entities, adherence to Code+ commitments is subject to independent annual auditing, and the Code serves as a relevant benchmark for assessing compliance with the risk assessment framework set out in Article 35 DSA. Comprehensive self-assessment reports from all 12 signatories have been made publicly available alongside the monitoring exercise results, in keeping with the Code+'s transparency and accountability commitments.

rows.title: EU AI Office publishes two reports on AI adoption and policymaking on first anniversary of AI Continent Action Plan
rows.summary:
The EU AI Office has published a report on AI adoption in public administration and a Joint Research Centre Science for Policy brief on a decade of EU AI policymaking, marking one year since the European Commission launched its AI Continent Action Plan.

On 9 April 2026, the European Commission published the two documents to coincide with the first anniversary of the AI Continent Action Plan, which is structured around five pillars: computing infrastructure, data, skills, AI adoption, and simplification of AI rules. No fines are associated with this publication.

The AI Office reports that, over the past twelve months, delivery against the Plan has included 19 AI Factories now operational alongside 13 AI Factory antennas, and a call for expression of interest on AI Gigafactories that attracted 76 responses across 60 sites in 16 Member States. The Commission further notes the publication of its Data Union Strategy in November, focused on unlocking data for AI development, and the launch of the European Legal Gateway Office in February 2026 during the AI Impact Summit in New Delhi as part of efforts to attract AI talent. The Apply AI Strategy, published in October 2025, is recorded as having generated dozens of calls worth up to one billion euros across strategic sectors. The Commission also notes its November proposal for an AI Omnibus aimed at simplifying rules and providing legal certainty, alongside the launch of an AI Act Service Desk to assist businesses and organisations in implementing the AI Act.

The report on advancing AI adoption in EU public administration, aligned with the fourth pillar of the Plan, presents a framework structured around three core activities: anchoring AI adoption in EU policies, regulations and principles; adapting the capabilities of public administrations; and applying AI in high-impact domains. The Joint Research Centre's Science for Policy brief, the AI Office states, provides an interpretative analysis of EU AI policymaking since 2018, drawing on collaboration with EU institutions and Member States. The brief finds that the EU has progressively constructed a comprehensive approach intended to foster innovation and competitiveness while safeguarding fundamental rights, market integrity and trust, rather than relying on a single policy instrument.

rows.title: CERT-EU Threat Landscape Report 2025: 174 Threat Actors Tracked, Nine Significant Incidents Responded to Across EU Institutions
rows.summary:
CERT-EU's annual Threat Landscape Report 2025, published on 8 April 2026, finds that the cyber threat environment facing Union entities grew more complex in 2025, with threat actors diversifying methods, expanding targets, and increasing use of artificial intelligence in their operations.

On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU, the cybersecurity body serving EU institutions, bodies and agencies, released its Threat Landscape Report 2025, covering cyber threats observed and incidents handled throughout the year. The report does not carry a monetary penalty but constitutes an authoritative assessment of the threat environment relevant to EU institutional cybersecurity obligations under Regulation (EU) 2023/2841.

CERT-EU reports that it tracked 174 threat actors in 2025, an increase from 110 in the prior year, and determines that Union entities had critical exposure to five of those actors. CERT-EU finds that cyberespionage and prepositioning remained the dominant motive, accounting for 38% of observed activity. CERT-EU states it responded to nine significant incidents during the year, seven of which the authority attributes to vulnerability exploitation, including two zero-day vulnerabilities. CERT-EU reports that 198 software products used by Union entities were targeted, representing an 80% increase compared to 2024, and identifies edge devices, including firewalls, VPNs and network appliances, as the highest-impact entry points.

CERT-EU finds that social engineering techniques expanded beyond email channels in 2025, with voice phishing, AI-generated deepfakes, OAuth abuse and ClickFix attacks all recorded as growing vectors. For the first time, the report analyses threats directed at 90 partner organisations, with CERT-EU determining that public administrations accounted for 60% of that recorded activity. CERT-EU notes that global events, including elections, armed conflicts and sanctions, continued to fuel cyber operations against Union entities and their broader ecosystem. The report includes ten prioritised recommendations covering measures such as phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication and end-to-end encryption for sensitive communications, alongside strategic foresight directed at the 2026 threat environment.

rows.title: HaDEA publishes overview of EU-funded AI projects under Horizon Europe, Digital Europe and EU4Health programmes
rows.summary:
The European Health and Digital Executive Agency has published an overview of its portfolio of AI-related projects funded under the Horizon Europe, Digital Europe and EU4Health programmes, detailing how those projects contribute to the European Commission's stated goal of positioning the EU as a hub for human-centric and reliable artificial intelligence under the AI Act framework.

On 8 April 2026, the European Health and Digital Executive Agency (HaDEA) published the overview, which sets out the Commission's investment of EUR 200 billion to boost AI development across Europe, including EUR 1 billion annually allocated to the Horizon Europe and Digital Europe programmes. The publication references the AI Act, adopted in 2024 as the world's first comprehensive legal framework on AI, and notes the establishment of the European AI Office to support coherent implementation and enforcement of that regulation.

HaDEA's overview notes that the agency funds and manages hundreds of AI-related projects spanning healthcare diagnostics, manufacturing efficiency, earth observation and agricultural sustainability. Among the projects highlighted is COMPASS AI, co-funded under the EU4Health programme and launched in October 2025. HaDEA states that COMPASS AI brings together clinicians, patients, AI developers, regulators and policymakers to identify challenges and develop evidence-based guidelines for AI deployment in clinical settings. The agency notes the project will validate those guidelines through four pilot studies conducted in real-life clinical environments and will deliver policy recommendations to the Commission aimed at accelerating AI adoption in healthcare at scale.

HaDEA also highlights the concluded Horizon Europe project PRAESIIDIUM, which the agency states combined mechanistic biomedical modelling with AI and real-world electronic health records to enable personalised prediction of prediabetes risk. The agency notes that PRAESIIDIUM used machine learning to produce a faster version of an existing physiological model capable of predicting glucose levels with equivalent accuracy over longer timeframes while requiring reduced computing resources. HaDEA further states the project collected data from a Canadian primary care database covering more than 300,000 patients to develop models predicting individual risk of prediabetes or diabetes from electronic health records. The publication also references the AI Continent Action Plan, adopted in April 2025, and the Apply AI Strategy as the policy frameworks within which these funded projects operate.
rows.body_name: European Health and Digital Executive Agency

rows.title: CERT-EU Publishes Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework for EU Institutions and Bodies
rows.summary:
CERT-EU has published its Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework, establishing the analytical and operational standards the body uses to classify, assess, and prioritise malicious cyber activities affecting European Union institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies and their ecosystem.

On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU released the framework under TLP:CLEAR classification, making it available to its constituents and the broader public. CERT-EU states that the framework sets out a shared reference model intended to support consistent reporting, alerting, and awareness-raising on cyber threat intelligence across Union entities and their designated operational contacts, including primary operational contacts (POCs) and local cybersecurity officers (LCOs).

CERT-EU states that the framework introduces core concepts including malicious activities of interest (MAIs), ecosystem mapping, threat and counter-threat categories, threat domains, threat levels, threat actor levels, tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs), and scoring mechanisms for adversaries and mitigation. CERT-EU defines an MAI as any adversarial cyber activity with a potential impact for Union entities or their ecosystem, encompassing confirmed compromise, suspicious attempts, adversarial resource development, and reconnaissance activities. CERT-EU notes that all components are aligned with recognised intelligence and cybersecurity standards, including practices drawn from EU cybersecurity regulations, FIRST, NATO, and threat intelligence industry good practices.

The framework also introduces the concept of an ecosystem to address threat visibility beyond constituent networks alone. CERT-EU determines that limiting monitoring to activity within constituent networks would risk missing relevant threats, while attempting to analyse all malicious activity across cyberspace would exceed available resources. CERT-EU defines the ecosystem concept as a set of components reflecting constituent exposure across countries of operation, sectors of activity, geopolitical events of interest, partners, providers, and systems and software. CERT-EU notes that targeting any of these components, for example by breaching a cloud service provider or managed service provider, can affect Union entities through service disruption, loss of data confidentiality, or malicious access to systems. CERT-EU states that the framework may evolve in response to regulatory changes and stakeholder feedback.

rows.title: BEREC Publishes Early Assessment of European Commission's Digital Networks Act Proposal, Identifying Concerns Over Centralisation and Legal Uncertainty
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications has published an early assessment of the European Commission's proposed Digital Networks Act, welcoming its updated objectives whilst identifying concerns over centralisation, the Single Passport mechanism, spectrum management, and end-user protections.

On 30 March 2026, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications published its formal early assessment of the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Networks Act. No financial penalty is associated with this assessment. The document sets out BEREC's analysis across multiple regulatory domains, from general authorisation and spectrum management to universal service and institutional governance.

BEREC finds that the proposed Single Passport mechanism, which concentrates enforcement responsibilities in the Member State of first notification, risks forum shopping, uneven supervisory burdens and slower enforcement. BEREC determines that the proposal could introduce greater operational complexity, administrative burden and legal uncertainty without offering clear benefits over the existing authorisation regime. On spectrum management, BEREC finds that the proposed single market procedure, which includes a Commission veto, represents a notable trend towards centralisation. BEREC further notes that proposals for unlimited licence duration and automatic renewals risk favouring existing rights holders and reducing market contestability. BEREC supports EU coordination on satellite authorisation but states that alignment with ITU-R rules is required.

Regarding the Significant Market Power regime, BEREC welcomes its preservation alongside symmetric access tools, but finds that proposals including new sequencing rules for remedies, the potential removal of the Recommendation on Relevant Markets, and expanded Commission veto powers could reduce the ability of national regulatory authorities to address national market needs. On numbering resources, BEREC finds that key concepts such as an EU-wide numbering plan and pan-European numbering ranges require further clarification, and that additional analysis of demand, use cases and operational impacts is needed. BEREC also finds that the narrowing of end-user protection scope and limitations on Member States' ability to maintain or introduce stricter measures pose risks to consumer rights, accessibility and affordability.

On institutional matters, BEREC acknowledges the assignment of new roles to it under the draft legislation, including contributing to EU-level merger assessments and issuing guidance on ecosystem cooperation. BEREC states that proposed institutional changes must not dilute the existing technical expertise and autonomy underpinning the current two-tier structure, and that any reinforcement of the Office of Digital Networks' support function is required to enhance, rather than limit, the collective autonomy of national regulatory authorities.
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.title: CJEU Rules Directive 2012/29/EU on Victims' Rights Inapplicable Where Criminal Offence Occurred Outside EU Jurisdiction
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled, in Case C-239/24 (Aurnois), that Directive 2012/29/EU establishing minimum standards on the rights, support and protection of victims of crime does not apply where the underlying criminal offence was committed outside the European Union and proceedings are conducted under a Member State's extraterritorial jurisdiction.

On 26 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Third Chamber) delivered its judgment in response to a request for a preliminary ruling from the Cour d'appel de Bruxelles, made by decision of 12 December 2023. The case concerns proceedings initiated in Belgium by FP and LD, family members of SX, a Saudi national and women's rights activist, against several leaders of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for crimes against humanity allegedly committed against her. No financial penalties were at issue in this matter.

The Court finds that the central question concerns the interpretation of Article 2(1)(a)(i) of Directive 2012/29/EU, which defines a 'victim' as a natural person who has suffered harm directly caused by a criminal offence. The Court notes that Recital 13 of the Directive expressly states that the instrument applies in relation to criminal offences committed in the Union and to criminal proceedings that take place in the Union, and that it confers rights on victims of extraterritorial offences only in relation to criminal proceedings taking place in the Union. The Court determines that this framing is material to the scope of the definition of 'victim' within the meaning of Article 2(1)(a).

The Court further finds that Directive 2012/29/EU was adopted on the legal basis of Article 82(2) TFEU, which confers EU legislative competence to establish minimum rules in criminal matters to the extent necessary to facilitate mutual recognition of judgments and judicial cooperation having a cross-border dimension. The Court determines that where a Member State exercises extraterritorial criminal jurisdiction over offences committed entirely outside the Union, the legislative competence underpinning the Directive does not extend to require that Member State to apply the Directive's victim-protection standards in such proceedings. The Court accordingly rules that Directive 2012/29/EU is inapplicable in circumstances where the criminal offence forming the basis of proceedings was not committed within the territory of the European Union.

rows.title: CJEU Rules Article 15 GDPR Access Requests Made Solely to Engineer Compensation Claims May Be Refused as Abusive
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that a first request for access to personal data under Article 15 GDPR may be refused as abusive where the controller demonstrates the request was made to artificially create conditions for a compensation claim under Article 82 GDPR, rather than to verify lawfulness of processing.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-526/24 (Brillen Rottler) in response to a reference from the Local Court, Arnsberg, Germany. The case concerned an individual who subscribed to the newsletter of Brillen Rottler, an optician, thirteen days before submitting an access request under Article 15 GDPR. Brillen Rottler refused the request, citing evidence that the individual had systematically subscribed to newsletters of multiple companies before lodging access requests and subsequent compensation claims.

The Court finds that a first access request may qualify as abusive within Article 12(5) GDPR where the controller demonstrates the request was made not to verify lawfulness of processing but to artificially engineer conditions for compensation. The Court determines that relevant circumstances include whether the data subject provided personal data without obligation to do so, the time elapsed between provision and the access request, and the overall conduct of the data subject. The Court states that numerous access requests to various controllers followed by compensation claims may be considered in establishing abusive intent.

The Court further finds that a data subject claiming compensation under Article 82 GDPR for infringement of the right of access is required to demonstrate actual material or non-material damage. The Court determines that compensation cannot be awarded where the data subject's own conduct is the determining cause of the damage alleged.

rows.title: CJEU Rules First Access Request Cannot Be Refused as Excessive Under GDPR
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that a controller cannot refuse a first-time access request under Article 15 GDPR on grounds of being excessive, and that a data subject may claim compensation for non-material damage in the form of loss of control over personal data.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Fourth Chamber) delivered its judgment in Case C-526/24, Brillen Rottler GmbH & Co. KG v TC, following a request for a preliminary ruling lodged by the Amtsgericht Arnsberg on 31 July 2024. The proceedings arose from an optician company's refusal to provide access to an individual's personal data, with the company contending that the request was excessive within the meaning of Article 12(5) GDPR.

The Court finds that Article 12(5) GDPR, read in conjunction with Article 15(1) GDPR, does not permit a controller to refuse a first access request by characterising it as excessive. The Court determines that the concept of an "excessive" request presupposes a repetitive character or manifest lack of good faith, neither of which can be established on the basis of a single, initial request alone. The Court further determines that a controller seeking to invoke excessive character bears the burden of demonstrating that the request manifestly meets that threshold.

On liability, the Court addresses Article 82(1) GDPR and finds that unlawful refusal of an access request may, in principle, give rise to non-material damage in the form of loss of control over personal data, as contemplated by recital 85 GDPR. The Court notes that the mere infringement of the GDPR does not automatically establish the existence of damage: the data subject is required to demonstrate that actual non-material damage was suffered as a result of the infringement. The Court affirms that compensation under Article 82(1) GDPR serves a compensatory rather than punitive function, and that national courts must assess the existence and extent of damage in each case.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: Unlicensed Sports Betting Operator May Owe Refunds to German Player
rows.summary:
Advocate General Nicholas Emiliou of the Court of Justice of the European Union has issued an Opinion in Case C-530/24 (Tipico), finding that EU law does not preclude Germany from imposing civil-law consequences, including refund obligations, on a sports betting operator that offered services without the required national licence.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published the Advocate General's Opinion in Case C-530/24, arising from a German consumer's claims against Maltese operator Tipico to recover stakes wagered and lost on Tipico's German website between 2013 and 9 October 2020. Tipico held a Maltese licence at the relevant time but not a German licence, as required under German law.

The Advocate General finds that a Member State licensing requirement for the provision of gambling services is compatible with the freedom to provide services, and that national authorities, including civil courts, are entitled to enforce that requirement against operators providing unlicensed services. The Advocate General finds that courts may apply civil-law consequences provided for under national law, including nullity of contracts with clients, which may give rise to an obligation to refund stakes. The Advocate General finds that such consequences are proportionate to the consumer-protection objective and contribute to dissuading operators from circumventing licensing systems.

The Advocate General identifies one exception. Where authorised sources within national authorities provided an operator with precise, unconditional and consistent assurances that the licensing requirement would not be enforced, the Advocate General finds it would be disproportionate to impose civil-law consequences for breach of that requirement. The Advocate General finds that in such circumstances, no fault could be imputed to the operator, and any liability for consumer harm could only be borne by the public authorities that gave those assurances. The Advocate General confirms that whether such assurances were in fact given is a matter for the German Federal Court of Justice to determine. The Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Amendment Proposals
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted amendments to the Network and Information Security 2 Directive, identifying areas of support and recommendations to align the proposals with data protection requirements under the GDPR and Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor published their Joint Opinion following a formal consultation request from the Commission dated 21 January 2026. The Commission's underlying cybersecurity package was published on 20 January 2026 with the stated aims of strengthening cybersecurity across the EU and reducing compliance burdens for organisations.

Regarding the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal, the EDPB and EDPS express support for the reinforced role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), the facilitation of cybersecurity certification uptake, and measures addressing risks to ICT supply chains. The Joint Opinion welcomes the provision under which ENISA's advice would be issued upon a prior request from the EDPB, and recommends that the EDPS also be designated as a possible requestor of such advice. The EDPB and EDPS find that the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification, and that ENISA is required to consult the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to the security of processing of personal data.

On the proposed NIS2 amendments, the EDPB and EDPS welcome the designation of European Digital Identity Wallet and European Business Wallet providers as essential entities. Consistent with their earlier Joint Opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation Proposal, the EDPB and EDPS express support for a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications, and find that such a mechanism would reduce administrative burden for notifying organisations without diminishing the level of protection afforded to individuals.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: EU Member States May Exclude Telecoms Equipment on National Security Grounds
rows.summary:
Advocate General Tamara Ćapeta has issued an Opinion in Case C-354/24 (Elisa Eesti) proposing that the Court of Justice of the European Union find that Member States may exclude telecommunications hardware and software where the manufacturer poses a national security risk, provided any exclusion remains subject to judicial review and proportionality assessment under EU law.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published Advocate General Ćapeta's Opinion following a request for a preliminary ruling from the Administrative Court, Tallinn, in proceedings brought by Estonian telecommunications provider Elisa Eesti AS. In 2022, Elisa Eesti applied for authorisation to use hardware and software from Huawei in its 2G-4G and 5G networks. The Estonian authorities refused, classifying Huawei as a high-risk manufacturer, and Elisa Eesti challenged that decision.

The Advocate General proposes that Member States may exclude third-country telecommunications equipment manufacturers on national security grounds. However, the Advocate General finds that any such decision is required to be subject to judicial review, including proportionality assessment. The Advocate General finds that a decision cannot rest on general suspicion and must involve a specific assessment of the intended equipment and associated risks. The Advocate General notes that the European Electronic Communications Code provides for security requirements for national telecommunications networks, and that competent authorities may draw on risk assessments conducted by EU institutions and other bodies, including the Commission's 2023 Communication on the Implementation of the 5G Cybersecurity Toolbox.

On property rights, the Advocate General observes that a restriction on equipment use on security grounds constitutes a limitation on use of property within Article 17(1) of the Charter of Fundamental Rights. The Advocate General states that a company is not entitled to compensation unless the national court finds the burden arising from the restriction is disproportionately heavy. The Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice, which will deliver judgment at a later date.

rows.title: CJEU Rules Police May Collect Biometric Data Only Where Strictly Necessary
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that national police authorities may collect biometric data during criminal investigations only where strictly necessary under Article 10 of Directive 2016/680, and that every such decision requires a stated reason.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment following a reference for a preliminary ruling from the Court of Appeal, Paris. The case arose from the detention in May 2020 of an individual, HW, who was held in Paris for organising an unnotified demonstration. During police custody, HW refused to consent to the collection of fingerprints and photographs, leading to his conviction and a fine of EUR 300, even though he was acquitted of the underlying offence.

The Court finds that biometric data, including fingerprints and photographs, constitute sensitive personal data attracting enhanced protection under EU law. The Court determines that reasonable grounds for suspecting an offence do not alone justify collecting such data. The Court states that every decision to gather identification data must contain a clear statement of reasons, enabling the data subject to understand the basis for the measure and to exercise the right to an effective remedy. The Court finds that national legislation rendering such collection systematic, without permitting the competent authority to assess necessity in each case, is contrary to EU law.

On the question of penalties for refusal, the Court holds that lawfulness depends on whether the underlying collection satisfies the strictly necessary condition. The Court finds that a criminal penalty for refusal is not incompatible with EU law, provided the penalty observes the principle of proportionality as laid down by the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

rows.title: EDPS Publishes Compass Document Setting Out AI Act Supervisory Approach for EU Institutions, 2026–2027
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its Compass document outlining how it will exercise its role as market surveillance authority and notified body for AI systems used or put into service by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act).

On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published the document titled "Towards Trustworthy AI in the EU Public Administration: The EDPS Compass for its New Role under the AI Act 2026–2027", setting out the authority's strategic approach to supervising EU institutions' AI systems. The EDPS states that the document is published expressly in the EDPS's capacity as market surveillance authority under the AI Act, and not in its capacity as a data protection authority.

The EDPS notes that its expanded mandate under the AI Act builds on earlier preparatory steps, including the publication of its AI Preparedness Strategy in May 2024 and the establishment of a dedicated Artificial Intelligence Unit in October 2024. The EDPS identifies four strategic pillars guiding the authority's action: supervision of EU institutions' AI systems, including both ex ante supervision as a notified body conducting conformity assessments of high-risk AI systems and ex post supervision as a market surveillance authority; contribution to AI Act governance and regulatory coordination, including participation in the AI Board; institutional empowerment for trustworthy AI, encompassing interinstitutional knowledge exchange and the establishment of a Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence; and international engagement and exchange of best practices.

The EDPS states that the two-year timeframe of 2026 to 2027 reflects two specific factors: the evolving nature of the AI Act, which the authority notes is to be complemented by harmonised standards and European Commission guidelines, and the possible amendments to the AI Act envisaged under the 2025 Digital Omnibus legislative proposal (COM(2025) 836 final), which the authority finds may require adjustments to the current supervisory setup. The EDPS states that it intends to pursue its supervisory responsibilities grounded in principles of impartiality, integrity, transparency, pragmatism and responsible innovation, with the stated objective of enhancing legal and procedural clarity in the implementation of the AI Act through a cooperative approach with EU institutions.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act Data Protection Safeguards
rows.summary:
