The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act, identifying areas where the proposal requires stronger data protection safeguards for health and genetic data in clinical trials.

On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the Commission's Proposal for a European Biotech Act. The proposal seeks to streamline the regulatory framework and update rules under the Clinical Trials Regulation (CTR).

The EDPB and EDPS support the proposal's objective of addressing fragmentation in CTR application and welcome establishing a single legal basis for personal data processing by sponsors and investigators. However, the EDPB and EDPS find that the sensitivity of health and genetic data processed in clinical trials requires a high standard of protection, and the joint opinion sets out recommendations to ensure proposed simplifications do not lower protections for trial participants.

The EDPB and EDPS find the proposal is required to specify whether actors involved in funding and conducting clinical trials act as sole or joint data controllers. On data retention, the authorities find the mandatory 25-year minimum retention period is required to apply only to the clinical trial master file, not to all personal data processed during a trial. Regarding further processing of trial data for other clinical trials or scientific research, the EDPB and EDPS find the Biotech Act is required to clearly define purposes and safeguards for such processing. The EDPB and EDPS also find the Biotech Act is required to ensure sponsor obligations complement existing AI Act requirements. On technical and organisational measures, the authorities find the CTR is required to explicitly mandate pseudonymisation where directly identifiable personal data need not be processed.

rows.title: BEREC Welcomes European Commission Digital Networks Act Proposal, Confirms Advisory Role in Legislative Process
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) has issued a statement welcoming the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Networks Act (DNA) and confirming its intention to provide technical expertise and advice throughout the forthcoming legislative process.

On 22 January 2026, BEREC published a press release stating its position on the Commission's DNA proposal, which consolidates several existing instruments including the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC), the BEREC Regulation, the Open Internet Regulation, the ePrivacy Directive and the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme. No fines were imposed; the statement concerns a legislative development rather than an enforcement action.

BEREC finds the DNA proposal represents a transition in the EU regulatory framework, aimed at ensuring fitness-for-purpose in light of technical and market developments. BEREC notes the proposal introduces provisions intended to facilitate the transition to fibre, high-quality 5G and future network technologies while preserving effective competition. BEREC's statement indicates it shares the Commission's assessment that core principles of the EECC, in particular ex-ante market regulation, are required to be maintained where market failures persist, on the basis that this approach ensures regulatory predictability and safeguards end-user welfare and rights. BEREC further states the future regulatory framework is required to respond to developments including digitalisation, virtualisation of network functions, integration of cloud solutions into networks, and the need to strengthen security and resilience.

BEREC confirms it will conduct a thorough examination of the Commission's proposals and will remain available to provide advice and support to legislators. As part of its engagement, BEREC notes it will hold its annual Stakeholder Forum on 31 March 2026, at which Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen will deliver a keynote address and panellists will discuss the evolution of the regulatory framework in light of the DNA proposal. BEREC additionally plans a public workshop in the first quarter of 2026 on migration to fibre-optic networks and copper switch-off, covering competitive dynamics following switch-off and the needs of vulnerable end-users, with the date and format to be confirmed.
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.title: CJEU Rules Body Camera Footage in Ticket Inspections Constitutes Direct Data Collection Under Art. 13 GDPR
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled in Case C-422/24 (Storstockholms Lokaltrafik) that personal data obtained through body cameras during ticket inspections constitutes data collected directly from the data subject, triggering the immediate information obligations set out in Art. 13 GDPR, rather than the indirect collection obligations under Art. 14 GDPR.

On 18 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its preliminary ruling in response to questions referred by a Swedish national court concerning a dispute between a Stockholm public transport company and the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY). IMY had fined the company for breaching several GDPR provisions, including the obligation to provide adequate information to passengers filmed by ticket inspectors equipped with body cameras. The fine amount in local currency and euro equivalent was not disclosed in the press release issued by the Court.

The Court finds that the classification of data collection as direct does not require the data subject to knowingly provide data or take any particular action. The Court determines that data obtained by observing a data subject is to be regarded as collected directly from that person, placing the processing squarely within Art. 13 GDPR. By contrast, the Court notes that indirect collection under Art. 14 GDPR applies only where the controller is not in direct contact with the data subject and obtains the data from a third-party source. The company had argued that its collection was indirect, a classification that would have altered both the timing and scope of its transparency obligations and, in its view, rendered the fine unjustified.

The Court further finds that the Art. 13 GDPR information obligation in cases of direct collection may be fulfilled through a multi-layered approach, consistent with European Data Protection Board Guidelines 3/2019 on the processing of personal data through video devices, adopted on 29 January 2020. The Court determines that the most important information, including the identity and contact details of the controller, the purposes and legal basis of the processing, data recipients, retention periods, and data subject rights of access and erasure, may be indicated on a warning sign, while remaining mandatory information is required to be provided in an easily accessible place. The Court does not itself resolve the underlying national dispute, which is referred back to the Swedish court for determination in accordance with this ruling.

rows.title: BEREC Adopts Strategy 2026–2030 and Work Programme 2026, Covering Connectivity, Competition and Digital Networks Act Preparation
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications has adopted its Strategy 2026–2030 and accompanying Work Programme 2026, consolidating its previous strategic frameworks and setting priorities around connectivity, competition, open digital ecosystems and end-user empowerment across the European Union.

On 9 December 2025, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) adopted the BEREC Strategy 2026–2030, which updates and consolidates three preceding strategies, namely the Strategy 2021–2025, the Medium-Term Strategy for Relations with Other Institutions 2022–2025 and the Medium-Term Strategy for International Cooperation 2022–2025, together with the Action Plan 2030. BEREC notes that no financial penalties are associated with this adoption. BEREC notes that the strategy may be revised ahead of 2030 if substantial regulatory changes occur, particularly in relation to its mandate and the forthcoming review of the legal framework.

BEREC determines that the new strategic cycle will be implemented under a renewed mandate. BEREC identifies priorities including the expansion of gigabit broadband connectivity, the rollout of 5G across all EU regions, and reinforcing end-user empowerment in electronic communications. BEREC notes that the Work Programme 2026, adopted by the Board of Regulators, builds on mandatory tasks set out in the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC) and anticipates work related to the forthcoming Digital Networks Act proposal. BEREC states its commitment to providing advice to the European Parliament, the Council and the European Commission on electronic communications and digital services, and to enhancing the consistent and harmonised application of regulation to develop the internal market.

Alongside the strategy adoption, BEREC approved a draft report on switching and termination of contracts for public consultation, with stakeholder contributions invited by 20 January 2026. BEREC states that the draft report examines the switching process, identifies disincentives and challenges for end users, and addresses applicable end-user rights throughout that process. BEREC also launched for public consultation updated Guidelines on Geographical Surveys of Broadband Network Deployment, in support of national regulatory authorities applying Art. 22 EECC, with contributions expected by 9 February 2026. BEREC finds that the updated guidelines aim to provide clearer guidance, foster greater harmonisation and improve comparability of survey results across Member States. The chairmanship of BEREC passes from Mr Robert Mourik of COMREG to Mr Marko Mišmaš of AKOS for 2026.

rows.title: FRA Paper Sets Out Fundamental Rights Framework for Live Facial Recognition Technology in EU Law Enforcement
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights published a focus paper examining the fundamental rights implications of live facial recognition technology used by law enforcement and border-management authorities across EU member states, with reference to protections under the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights and applicable data protection law.

On 27 November 2019, the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights released the paper, which analyses the deployment of facial recognition technology, defined as the comparison of digital facial images to determine whether they depict the same person, in law enforcement contexts. The Agency notes that no fines were imposed and no monetary penalties are associated with this publication.

The Agency finds that examples of national law enforcement authorities in the EU actively using live facial recognition technology remain sparse, though several authorities are testing its potential. The paper focuses specifically on live facial recognition, which the Agency describes as the comparison of footage obtained from video surveillance cameras with images held in databases. The Agency finds that facial images constitute sensitive data under EU law, given their status as a form of biometric data, and notes that such images are also relatively easy to capture in public spaces without the knowledge of individuals concerned.

The Agency finds that accuracy of matching is improving but that the risk of errors remains real, particularly for certain minority groups, raising concerns under equality and non-discrimination provisions. The paper further identifies as a central fundamental rights challenge the fact that individuals whose images are captured and processed may be unaware that processing is occurring, and are therefore unable to challenge potential misuses. The Agency finds this inability to contest processing raises concerns relating to the rights to privacy, data protection, and effective remedy. The paper situates these considerations within broader EU frameworks governing biometric data processing and the use of technology in law enforcement, including the Law Enforcement Directive (Directive 2016/680), which governs the processing of personal data by competent authorities for law enforcement purposes.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: Prior Judicial Authorisation Not Required for Competition Email Seizures
rows.summary:
Advocate General Medina has opined that national competition authorities are not required to obtain prior judicial authorisation before seizing business emails during competition investigations, provided adequate procedural safeguards and subsequent judicial review are in place.

On 23 October 2025, Advocate General Laila Medina delivered a supplementary Opinion in three joined cases (C-258/23, C-259/23 and C-260/23) arising from seizures of employee business emails conducted by the Portuguese Competition Authority. The companies under investigation contested the seizures, arguing that correspondence secrecy as a fundamental right required authorisation from an investigating judge rather than the Public Prosecutor's Office.

Advocate General Medina distinguished the present cases from the Grand Chamber judgment in Bezirkshauptmannschaft Landeck (C-548/21), which held that access to personal data on a mobile telephone for criminal investigations is subject to prior judicial review. Advocate General Medina found that competition authority seizures target business information concerning legal persons and do not afford uncontrolled access to a comprehensive picture of private life in the manner that mobile telephone access does.

Regarding the interference with the fundamental right to protection of personal data under Article 8 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights, Advocate General Medina found that the principle of proportionality is complied with where certain procedural safeguards are ensured, in addition to obligations under the General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679, and subject to subsequent judicial review both during and at the close of investigation. Advocate General Medina found that prior judicial authorisation would be required only where seizures are carried out at a person's private residence or to incriminate a natural person under criminal law. Directive (EU) 2019/1 permits Member States to provide for a prior authorisation mechanism issued by a judicial authority. The Advocate General's Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice, which will deliver judgment at a later date.

rows.title: ENISA Threat Landscape 2025: Public Administration Most Targeted EU Sector, Ransomware Remains Leading Cybercrime Tool
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published its annual Threat Landscape report for 2025, identifying public administration as the most targeted sector in the EU and ransomware as the most impactful cybercrime tool, drawing on incident data collected across the reporting period.

On 24 September 2025, ENISA published the ENISA Threat Landscape 2025 Booklet (catalogue number TP-01-25-025-EN-N), covering the threat environment facing EU Member States and organisations. No fines or penalties are associated with this report.

ENISA finds that public administration accounted for 38.2% of all recorded incidents, with distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks representing 94.8% of incidents within that sector and ransomware particularly affecting municipalities. The transport sector ranked second at 7.5% of incidents, with air, logistics, and maritime subsectors identified as focus areas, the latter notably targeted by state-nexus intrusion sets. ENISA determines that essential entities represented 53.7% of the total number of recorded incidents across all sectors. ENISA notes that digital infrastructure and services, including Digital Services Providers, remain high-value targets despite accounting for only 2.2% of recorded incidents, particularly where cyber dependencies are leveraged as launchpads for follow-up attacks.

ENISA identifies phishing as the dominant initial intrusion vector, accounting for approximately 60% of cases and encompassing malspam, vishing, and malvertising. ENISA reports that vulnerability exploitation represented 21.3% of initial access vectors, with 68% of those cases leading to malware deployment as a follow-up activity. ENISA finds that ransomware recorded an 11% decrease compared to the previous Threat Landscape edition, yet remained the most impactful cybercrime tool, with the Akira and SafePay strains among the most deployed. ENISA reports that hacktivism dominated incident volume in the EU, driven primarily by low-impact DDoS campaigns, with only 2% of such incidents resulting in service disruption. ENISA attributes over 60% of hacktivist claims to the group NoName057(16), sustained through its DDoSia platform. ENISA reports that among state-nexus intrusion sets, APT28, APT29, and Sandworm are the most active in the EU, targeting public administration, defence, and telecommunications entities, while the Matryoshka Information Manipulation Set is identified as the most active actor in Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference campaigns. ENISA further finds that both state-aligned intrusion sets and cybercriminal operators are increasingly leveraging artificial intelligence for productivity and optimisation of malicious activities.

rows.title: Court of Justice Sets Aside General Court Ruling on EDPS Decision Against Single Resolution Board Over Pseudonymised Data Processing
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union (First Chamber) has set aside the General Court's judgment of 26 April 2023 that had annulled the European Data Protection Supervisor's revised decision finding that the Single Resolution Board failed to fulfil its obligations relating to the processing of personal data under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 4 September 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-413/23 P, ruling on an appeal brought by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) against the General Court's judgment in SRB v EDPS (T-557/20). The proceedings originated from five complaints (Cases 2019-947, 2019-998, 2019-999, 2019-1000 and 2019-1122) submitted by shareholders and creditors of a banking institution in the context of the Single Resolution Board's procedure for granting compensation following the resolution of that institution. The CJEU notes that no financial penalty was imposed in this matter.

The Court examined two core questions of interpretation under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. First, the Court considered the concept of 'personal data' under Art. 3(1) of that Regulation in the context of pseudonymised data transmitted to a third party. The Court's analysis addressed whether data pseudonymised by the SRB and transmitted to an external valuer retained the character of personal data in the hands of that third party, having regard to all means reasonably likely to be used to re-identify the natural persons concerned, including available technology and objective factors such as costs and time required for identification. Second, the Court addressed the concept of 'pseudonymisation' as defined in Art. 3(6) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, and the consequent obligations under Art. 15(1)(d) concerning the controller's obligation to inform data subjects of the recipients or categories of recipients of their personal data.

The Court finds that Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 is to be interpreted homogeneously with the GDPR where its provisions follow the same principles, consistent with recital 5 of that Regulation. The Court finds that the General Court erred in its assessment of the EDPS's revised decision of 24 November 2020, which the EDPS had determined found that the SRB failed to comply with its information obligations under Art. 15(1)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 when transmitting pseudonymised data to a third-party valuer. The EDPS was supported in the appeal by the European Data Protection Board as intervener, while the European Commission intervened in support of the SRB.

rows.title: ENISA publishes updated European Cybersecurity Skills Framework consolidating 12 professional role profiles for EU workforce alignment
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published updated materials for the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework (ECSF), consolidating 12 cybersecurity professional role profiles intended to serve as the EU reference point for defining and assessing cybersecurity skills across the public and private sectors.

On 26 June 2025, ENISA published the current state of the ECSF, a practical framework tool originally presented at the first ENISA Cybersecurity Skills Conference in September 2022. The framework carries no monetary penalty and involves no fine amounts. ENISA describes the ECSF as an open European tool designed to establish a common understanding of cybersecurity professional role profiles, alongside clear mappings to the skills, knowledge and competences required for each.

ENISA finds that the ECSF summarises cybersecurity-related roles into 12 distinct profiles, each individually analysed according to responsibilities, skills, synergies and interdependencies. The agency states that the framework facilitates recognition of cybersecurity skills and supports the design of cybersecurity-related training programmes. The Commission's April 2023 Communication on a Cybersecurity Skills Academy designated the ECSF as the basis upon which the Academy will define and assess relevant skills, monitor the evolution of skill gaps and provide indications on emerging needs.

ENISA notes that the 3rd Skills Conference, hosted under the Hungarian Presidency of the Council of the EU, confirmed the framework's maturity and versatility, demonstrating how the ECSF supports workforce planning aligned with obligations under the NIS2 Directive, adapts to the AI domain and enables skills attestation for cybersecurity professionals. The agency further states that the ECSF 2025 workshop showcased how EU co-funded projects are leveraging the framework across diverse sectors and use cases, supporting harmonisation of cybersecurity skills and professional roles across the EU. ENISA has also published a mapping between the ECSF and ESCO, the multilingual classification of European Skills, Competences and Occupations, as well as an application of the ECSF to the aviation sector developed by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency. ENISA notes that NIS2 obligations are mapped to ECSF roles within the accompanying documentation, and the framework is available in interactive, xlsx and JSON formats.

rows.title: CJEU Orders Bulgaria to Pay Lump Sum for Failure to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled against the Republic of Bulgaria in infringement proceedings brought by the European Commission for failure to transpose Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information, ordering payment of a lump sum under Article 260(3) TFEU.

On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) delivered its judgment in Case C-237/23, European Commission v Republic of Bulgaria. The Commission had initiated proceedings under Article 258 TFEU on 14 April 2023, seeking a declaration that Bulgaria had failed to adopt the legislative, regulatory and administrative provisions necessary to comply with Directive 2019/1024, or had at minimum failed to communicate those measures to the Commission, in breach of Article 17 of that Directive. The Commission also sought a lump sum calculated at EUR 1,800 per day from the expiry of the transposition deadline, subject to a minimum of EUR 504,000, and a penalty payment of EUR 9,720 per day for any continuing infringement beyond the date of judgment.

The Court finds that Bulgaria failed to fulfil its obligations under Article 17 of Directive 2019/1024, which required Member States to bring into force the necessary transposition measures. The Court notes that the Commission sought the higher of two lump sum amounts: the daily rate of EUR 1,800 multiplied by the number of days of infringement, or the minimum flat sum of EUR 504,000. The proceedings record a partial withdrawal by the Commission, indicating that Bulgaria had taken some transposition steps during the course of the litigation, though the Court's ruling confirms the infringement was established.

Directive 2019/1024, adopted on 20 June 2019, replaced Directive 2003/98/EC and updated the framework governing re-use of public sector information, including provisions addressing real-time access to dynamic data, extension of re-use obligations to public undertakings and research bodies, and considerations relevant to artificial intelligence applications. The Court finds that Bulgaria's failure to communicate complete transposition measures within the prescribed deadline engaged the lump sum mechanism under Article 260(3) TFEU. The judgment records that costs were also sought against Bulgaria by the Commission.

rows.title: Court of Justice Rules Netherlands Failed to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024, Orders Lump Sum Payment
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) has ruled that the Kingdom of the Netherlands failed to fulfil its obligations under Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information by not adopting and immediately communicating the necessary transposition measures, ordering the payment of a lump sum under Art. 260(3) TFEU.

On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-213/23, European Commission v Kingdom of the Netherlands. The infringement proceedings were brought by the European Commission under Art. 258 TFEU and Art. 260(3) TFEU, following the Netherlands' failure to transpose Directive 2019/1024 by the prescribed deadline and to communicate those measures to the Commission as required by Art. 17 of that Directive. The Commission had initially sought a lump sum calculated at EUR 13,900 per day from the expiry of the transposition deadline until the infringement was remedied, subject to a minimum lump sum of EUR 3,892,000, as well as a daily penalty of EUR 75,060 from the date of judgment if the infringement persisted.

The Court finds that the Netherlands failed to adopt the legislative, regulatory and administrative provisions necessary to comply with Directive 2019/1024 and failed to communicate those provisions to the Commission immediately, in breach of Art. 17 of the Directive. The judgment notes that the Commission filed a partial withdrawal, indicating that elements of the infringement had been remedied during the course of proceedings, a circumstance the Court takes into account in its assessment of the financial penalties to be imposed under Art. 260(3) TFEU.

Directive 2019/1024, adopted on 20 June 2019, establishes rules facilitating the re-use of public sector information across the European Union, including provisions on real-time access to dynamic data and the supply of high-value datasets. The Court finds that the Netherlands' incomplete transposition and failure to communicate transposition measures constituted a failure of a Member State to fulfil its obligations, warranting the imposition of a lump sum payment.

rows.title: CJEU Rules GDPR Does Not Permit National Case Law Requiring Prior Consultation of Data Subjects Before Disclosure of Official Documents
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union, sitting as the First Chamber, ruled on 3 April 2025 in Case C-710/23 (L.H. v Ministerstvo zdravotnictví) that the GDPR precludes national case law imposing an obligation on public authorities to inform and consult a data subject before disclosing official documents containing that person's personal data, where no such obligation is grounded in national legislative or regulatory provisions.

On 3 April 2025, the Court of Justice delivered its preliminary ruling following a reference from the Nejvyšší správní soud (Supreme Administrative Court, Czech Republic), lodged on 22 November 2023. The underlying dispute concerned a refusal by the Czech Ministry of Health to disclose to L.H. information identifying the natural-person representatives of legal persons named in contracts for the purchase of COVID-19 screening tests and in certificates relating to those tests. No fine was imposed in these proceedings.

The Court finds that the personal data of a natural person acting as the representative of a legal person falls within the material scope of the GDPR (Art. 4(1) GDPR), notwithstanding Recital 14, which states that the Regulation does not cover the processing of personal data concerning legal persons. The Court determines that where such data appears in official documents held by a public authority, its disclosure constitutes processing of personal data subject to the lawfulness requirements of Article 6 GDPR, and may be grounded in a legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) or a task carried out in the public interest (Art. 6(1)(e)).

The Court further finds that Article 86 GDPR permits Member States to reconcile public access to official documents with data protection rights, but that any procedural requirements imposed on controllers in that context, including obligations to notify or consult data subjects prior to disclosure, are required to have a basis in Union or Member State law. The Court determines that a procedural obligation created solely through national case law, without a corresponding legislative or regulatory foundation, is incompatible with the GDPR's requirement that processing be grounded in law. The Court notes that such judge-made obligations cannot substitute for the legislative reconciliation that Article 86 GDPR contemplates between transparency and data protection.

rows.title: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity for Smart Cars
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) published a good practices report on the security of smart cars, covering connected and semi-autonomous vehicles, with reference to existing standardisation, legislative and policy initiatives across the European Union.

On 25 November 2019, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity released its report titled "Good Practices for Security of Smart Cars", setting out a reference framework intended to promote cybersecurity for connected and automated vehicles across Europe. The report carries no fine or enforcement action, as it is a guidance document published for information purposes only and does not constitute a legal act of ENISA unless adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 2019/881.

ENISA identifies connected and semi-autonomous vehicles as presenting a distinct cybersecurity surface, encompassing in-vehicle systems, infrastructure and backend components. ENISA defines a high-level reference model for smart car architecture and sets out a threats taxonomy covering attack scenarios directed at vehicle networks, software, cloud systems and communications protocols. ENISA finds that threats to smart cars carry implications not only for data security but for physical safety, framing the core concern as "cybersecurity for safety".

ENISA catalogues the good practices across three categories: policies, organisational practices and technical practices. Under policies, ENISA identifies security by design, privacy by design, asset management and risk and threat management as foundational requirements. Organisational practices identified by ENISA include supplier relationship management, staff training and awareness, security management and incident management. Technical practices set out by ENISA span detection capabilities, protection of networks and protocols, software security, cloud security, cryptography, access control, self-protection and cyber resilience measures, and continuity of operations. ENISA notes that semi-autonomous systems require dedicated self-protection and resilience considerations given their operational characteristics.

The report draws on contributions from automotive manufacturers, national cybersecurity authorities including ANSSI and BSI, telecommunications providers, academic institutions and industry bodies, and is addressed to vehicle manufacturers, suppliers, infrastructure operators and policy makers involved in the connected vehicle ecosystem.

rows.title: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity and Resilience of Smart Cars
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Network and Information Security (ENISA) published a report in December 2016, cataloguing good practices and recommendations for securing smart cars against cyber threats, with ENISA identifying sensitive assets, corresponding threats, risks, mitigation factors and proposed security measures across three categories.

On 13 January 2017, the European Union Agency for Network and Information Security published the report titled "Cyber Security and Resilience of Smart Cars: Good Practices and Recommendations", dated December 2016. The report contains no fine or enforcement action and carries no EUR or USD equivalent. It is presented as a guidance document intended for car manufacturers, tier-one and tier-two suppliers, and aftermarket suppliers operating within the EU automotive sector.

ENISA identifies smart cars as presenting a particular cybersecurity challenge because, the agency notes, security measures in connected vehicles must also guarantee physical safety, a dependency that distinguishes this sector from conventional information security contexts. To develop its findings, ENISA engaged experts from industry, academia, law enforcement and national agencies across multiple EU member states, conducting interviews and a validation workshop held in Munich in October 2016. The agency groups its resulting good practices into three categories: policy and standards, organisational measures, and security functions.

The report maps the sensitive assets present within typical smart car architectures, which ENISA identifies as spanning powertrain control, chassis control, body control, infotainment control and communications systems. For each asset category, ENISA sets out the threat landscape and associated risks, and identifies mitigation factors and candidate security measures. ENISA states that the study draws on expertise gathered through its Cars and Roads Security expert group, known as CaRSEC. The agency notes that the publication represents the views and interpretations of its authors and editors and is intended for information purposes only, and that it does not necessarily represent the state of the art at the time of any subsequent reading. ENISA adds that it may update the publication from time to time.

rows.title: ENISA Hosts 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on 16 September 2025
rows.summary:
The EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) is convening its 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on 16 September 2025, focusing on NIS2 Directive implementation, the EU action plan for hospital cybersecurity, and the European Health Data Space (EHDS).

On 26 March 2025, ENISA announced the full-day physical event, organised in collaboration with the Romanian National Cyber Security Directorate (DNSC) and the European Cybersecurity Competence Centre (ECCC), and hosted at the Bucharest University of Economic Studies (ASE Bucharest), Aula Magna. No fines or enforcement determinations are associated with this announcement.

ENISA identifies the healthcare sector as facing persistent cybersecurity challenges, including ransomware, phishing campaigns and threats to medical devices and systems. The conference agenda addresses four thematic sessions: the current policy and legal framework, the evolving threat landscape and regional approaches, the EU action plan for the cybersecurity of hospitals and healthcare providers, and funding opportunities under the Digital Europe Programme. ENISA confirms speakers include a policy officer from the European Commission's DG CNECT covering NIS2 and the EHDS, a representative from the German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) on medical device threats and vulnerabilities, and senior officials from the cybersecurity agencies of Moldova and Ukraine addressing regional approaches.

ENISA notes that Session 3 will examine challenges and opportunities in implementing the EU action plan for hospitals and healthcare providers, with presentations from ENISA's Head of Resilience of Critical Sectors Unit and the Deputy Director of the DNSC. Session 4 will cover ECCC funding calls under the Digital Europe Programme and feature project presentations, including ASCLEPIUS, which ENISA describes as delivering cybersecurity managed services to SMEs in healthcare and the supply chain, and NERO, described as a cybersecurity awareness ecosystem for SMEs focused on patient data security. ENISA identifies the target audience as including healthcare IT professionals, clinical information security professionals, medical device manufacturers, national healthcare authorities, compliance and data privacy professionals, standardisation bodies, and academia.

rows.title: ENISA Publishes Governance Framework Outline for National Cybersecurity Strategies Across EU Member States
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published a governance framework outline for national cybersecurity strategies (NCSSs), presenting good practices and statistical findings to support EU Member States in structuring and implementing effective cybersecurity governance arrangements under the EU Cybersecurity Act (Regulation (EU) No 2019/881).

On 28 February 2023, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity released the publication titled "A Governance Framework for National Cybersecurity Strategies", authored by ENISA staff alongside consultancy Wavestone, with contributions from national authorities across more than a dozen EU Member States including Austria, Belgium, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Lithuania, Malta, the Netherlands, Slovakia, Spain, and Sweden. ENISA states that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

ENISA finds that effective governance frameworks for NCSSs require attention to several key elements, including clear institutional responsibilities, coordination mechanisms between relevant national bodies, and structured processes for the deployment and review of cybersecurity strategies. The publication identifies a collection of good practices that European countries have put in place to ensure these elements function effectively across current and future NCSSs. ENISA notes that the statistical outline is intended to link key findings to the proposed framework components and to illustrate how Member States have approached governance in practice.

The agency states that the publication is a lightweight companion to a fuller detailed study, "Building Effective Governance Frameworks for the Implementation of National Cybersecurity Strategies", to which readers are directed for comprehensive analysis. ENISA states that the publication represents the views and interpretations of the agency and is not to be construed as a legally binding act unless adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 2019/881. ENISA states that the document is made available free of charge for information purposes and reproduction is authorised provided the source is acknowledged.
